10.1.label: Question 10.1
10.1.scenario: The government of a country with a well-developed economy is worried that health insurance companies operating within that country will become insolvent, resulting in policyholders' claims not being met. Therefore it has decided that from now on all insurance premiums will be taxed at 10%. In return the government will set up a fund to pay all claims from companies that become insolvent.
10.1.question: Set out the advantages and disadvantages of this proposal for each of the main parties involved.

Advantages to the government: The cost of providing the guarantee is very unlikely to be as much as 10% of premiums, and so the government will receive extra revenue. [½]
The country could promote itself abroad as having a 'cast iron guarantee that all valid claims will be paid' resulting in growth in the health insurance sector of the country's economy. [½]
There will be fewer unhappy policyholders because all valid claims will be paid. [½]
Disadvantages to the government: Policyholders may go to other countries to obtain cover, particularly for corporate business, ... [½]
... resulting in a decline in the health insurance industry, unemployment etc. [½]
There is less of an incentive for health insurance companies to run their business prudently, charge adequate premiums and so on. [½]
This may result in higher levels of insolvency and hence high government payouts. [½]
Similarly, policyholders and brokers will pay less regard to financial strength and place business with the cheapest insurer. [½]
There is a potential for general adverse publicity (nobody likes paying higher taxes) and loss of goodwill with the health insurance industry. [½]
Policyholders may not take out insurance that they otherwise would have done (and need). [½]
There may be a greater burden on the State healthcare or welfare systems if fewer people take out health insurance as a result of higher premiums. [½]
It may be difficult to check that claims are all valid. [½]
The cost of collecting (and auditing) the tax may be high. [½]
Advantages to the policyholder: All valid claims will be paid. [½]
There is less need to consider the financial strength of a company, so potential customers could just go for the cheapest company, which will be easier for them. [½]
Disadvantages to the policyholder: Premiums will probably rise as a result of the tax. [½]
Policyholder may choose not to, or not be able to afford to, buy cover that they genuinely need. [½]
There may be less choice of cover if some insurers pull out of the market. [½]
Advantages to the industry: All valid claims will be paid so there is no need to organise other similar schemes. [½]
Sales could rise if the scheme is properly promoted. [½]
Disadvantages to the industry: Sales volumes may fall as a result of increased premiums (or there may be switching to cheaper products), ... [½]
... resulting in less premium income for insurance companies. [½]
Cost of revising premiums, collecting the tax, amending literature, promoting the scheme, etc. [½]
A greater focus on price when choosing products, and less on financial strength. [½]

10.2.label: Question 10.2
10.2.scenario: A particular jurisdiction operates 'dual regulation' of insurance companies, ie there are two independent regulators. One regulator is responsible for the prudential regulation of insurance companies; the other is responsible for the regulation of conduct in financial markets, specifically the fair treatment of customers. Another jurisdiction operates 'single regulation', ie there is one regulator that is responsible for all of these areas.
10.2.question: Discuss the relative merits of these arrangements.

Benefits of single regulation: This avoids unnecessary overlap or duplication of effort, so should reduce the cost of regulation for that country and for insurance companies. [½]
Overhead costs may also be reduced. [½]
The arrangement avoids problems that may arise if insurance companies find it difficult to understand which regulator to approach for different issues. [½]
It should also avoid problems that might arise if rules published separately by the two regulators appear to contradict each other. [½]

There may be times when the two aspects of regulation are in conflict with each other, eg updating CI insurance policy wording to payout on new illnesses might be deemed to treat policyholders more fairly, but could conflict with a prudential approach to ensuring ongoing solvency if premiums are guaranteed. Single regulation would require such conflicts to be resolved within the regulatory body, which should be more straightforward for insurance companies then to action. [1]
Benefits of dual regulation: It allows specialist focus in particular areas of regulation, thus making best use of resource expertise within the regulatory bodies. [½]
This should also result in more efficient analysis and actions by the regulator, ... [½]
... which in turn should provide better protection to companies / policyholders. [½]

It may be easier to implement consistency across all financial services providers, if the two regulators operate across banks, investment companies etc (ie not just insurance companies), specialising in type of regulation (ie prudential or market conduct) rather than type of company. [1]

Conflicts between the prudential and market conduct viewpoints would not be buried in internal discussions within the regulatory body, but brought to insurance companies in order for them to be part of the solution ... [1]
... thus giving them a greater stake in the decision-making process. [½]
